explanation_md: On the first check, `p` is `nullptr` so the else branch runs, printing `"empty"`. Then `p = &x` points `p` at `x`. The second check passes (`p != nullptr` is true) so `*p` dereferences to `x`'s value, 10.

options.a: The value 5 is stored at address 0 in memory â€” harmless
options.b: The compiler produces an error at compile time
options.c: Undefined behavior â€” typically a crash at runtime

explanation_md: A null pointer holds no valid address. Dereferencing it attempts to write to address 0 (or wherever nullptr maps on the platform), which is undefined behavior â€” the OS or hardware typically raises a fault and crashes the program. The compiler does not catch this at compile time because pointer validity is a runtime property.

prompt_md: A pointer `p` is non-null and points to an `int` that holds the value `0`. What does the condition `if (p != nullptr)` evaluate to?

options.a: false, because the pointed-to value is 0
options.b: true, because p holds a valid (non-null) address
options.c: false, because 0 is falsy in C++
options.d: A compile error because you cannot compare a pointer to nullptr

explanation_md: `p != nullptr` tests only whether `p` itself holds a valid address â€” it says nothing about the value stored at that address. Since `p` points to a real `int` object (even though that `int` is 0), the pointer is non-null and the condition is true.
`a` is -3, which is not greater than 0, so `getPtr` returns `nullptr`. The null check in `main` fails (`p == nullptr`), so the else branch runs and prints `"none"`.

options.a: You cannot print `*p` with `std::cout` when the type is `int*`
options.b: `*p` is dereferenced before `p` is set to `&x`, so it dereferences a null pointer
options.c: `int x = 7` must come before `int* p = nullptr`

explanation_md: The `std::cout << *p` line runs while `p` is still `nullptr`. Dereferencing a null pointer is undefined behavior and causes a crash. The fix is to move `p = &x` before the `std::cout` line, or add a null-check before dereferencing.
